evidence: Runtime dependency bags are very wide and duplicated across modules: `RuntimeContainerOps`/`RuntimeUrbitOps`/`Runtime*Ops` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, `RectifyRuntime` in `goseg/rectify/rectify.go`, `workflowRuntime` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, `importerRuntime` in `goseg/importer/importer.go`, and `StartupRuntime`/`startupInitRuntime` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`.

Multiple exported wrappers add little behavior beyond resolving defaults then delegating (for example `PublishSchedulePack`, `SchedulePackEvents`, `GetState`, `UpdateBroadcast` in `goseg/broadcast/state.go`; plus paired `WithContext`/`WithContextAndRuntime` entrypoints in `goseg/rectify/rectify.go`).

Test setup repeatedly re-creates large callback maps/struct overrides to isolate behavior (`testWireguardRuntime`, `testDockerRoutineRuntime`, `withStartBackgroundServicesRuntime`, `c2cTestRuntime`), signaling high indirection cost in production abstractions.

Section mutation APIs rely on runtime type assertions with `any` in both system and urbit config paths (`goseg/structs/configs.go` `UpdateSection` and `goseg/config/urbit.go` `UpdateUrbitSectionConfig`/`UrbitSectionMutator`), which shifts contract checks from compile-time to runtime.

Runtime dependency seams are split into many overlapping op structs (`RuntimeStartupOps`, `RuntimeConfigOps`, `RuntimeLoadOps`, `RuntimeServiceOps`) with repeated fields in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, increasing drift risk when runtime capabilities change.

Connection/session registry logic duplicates auth-vs-unauth traversal and mutation patterns (`findMuConn`, `removeConnectionByConn`, `removeClientFromOtherBucket`) while a generic bucket helper already exists, indicating unresolved structural duplication in `goseg/session/connection_registry.go`.

goseg/config/config_update_registry.go uses panic in configPatchFieldFromSpec when apply/target are missing, while sibling config parsing paths in goseg/config/config_update_schema.go consistently return typed errors.

goseg/importer/importer.go sets transition publishing to TransitionPublishBestEffort and repeatedly logs+continues on publish failures (e.g., runImportPhases Emit/OnError, finalizeImportedPierReadiness), but goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go defaults TransitionErrorPolicy to TransitionPublishStrict and returns transition publish failures.

goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go suppresses optional init/start/health errors (logOptionalStartupFailure then return nil), creating non-failing but degraded startup paths not reflected in return values.

findings.summary: Subsystem runtimes are oversized callback bags that duplicate responsibility boundaries instead of exposing cohesive ports.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` defines many partially-overlapping `Runtime*Ops` structs, then composes them again (`RuntimeTransitionOps`, `runtimeSeamRegistry`, `defaultRuntime*` builders).
`RectifyRuntime`, `workflowRuntime`, and `importerRuntime` each carry long lists of function pointers for config/container/event interactions, then run explicit validation for missing callbacks.
The same domains (container lifecycle, ship config mutation, transition publishing) are surfaced through different callback signatures in each runtime struct rather than a stable shared interface.
findings.suggestion: Refactor to a smaller set of cohesive domain ports (for example `ShipConfigPort`, `ContainerLifecyclePort`, `TransitionBusPort`) and make each subsystem depend on only the ports it uses; keep test seams at port boundaries rather than per-function callback fields.

findings.summary: Thin wrapper layers create extra call depth without adding policy or translation.
evidence: `broadcast/state.go` exports many top-level functions that only resolve runtime and forward (`PublishSchedulePack`, `SchedulePackEvents`, `UpdateScheduledPack`, `GetScheduledPack`, `UpdateBroadcast`, `GetState`).
`rectify.go` defines paired entrypoints (`XWithContext` -> `XWithContextAndRuntime`) that mostly forward after runtime resolution.
`wifi_runtime_api.go` includes wrappers like `DefaultWiFiRuntimeService` and runtime resolvers that mostly forward to `wifiRuntime` methods with minimal additional behavior.
findings.suggestion: Collapse duplicate forwarding layers by choosing one primary API style per module (instance-oriented or package-level), and keep wrappers only where they enforce explicit policy (caching, auth, retries, metrics).

findings.summary: Repeated resolve/default/validate scaffolding forms an internal framework that increases indirection cost across modules.
evidence: Each module re-implements a similar flow: construct defaults, merge overrides, validate callbacks, then delegate (`resolve*Runtime`, `withDefaults*`, `validate`).
This pattern appears in unrelated domains (bootstrap, rectify loops, ship workflow, importer, startup orchestration), but without a shared contract that reduces duplication.
Callers often pass partially populated runtime values, relying on hidden merging behavior instead of explicit constructor requirements.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on explicit constructors returning minimal runtime interfaces (required dependencies as parameters), and remove generic merge/validate scaffolding except at true composition boundaries.

findings.summary: Tests require large custom runtime builders, indicating production abstractions are too granular and expensive to substitute.
evidence: `testWireguardRuntime` initializes many nested runtime operation structs before a single behavior can be tested.
`testDockerRoutineRuntime` and `withStartBackgroundServicesRuntime` define extensive default callback maps/stubs just to make runtime validation pass.
`main_test.go` creates option helpers (`withC2cConnectivityState`, `withC2cDeviceState`, `withC2cModeState`) to patch multiple nested callback buckets for one scenario.
findings.suggestion: Introduce narrower test-facing seams aligned to use-cases (for example dedicated fakes for transition publish, ship status lookup, and startup task scheduling) so tests can substitute one dependency per behavior instead of constructing full runtime bags.

findings.summary: Config section mutation contracts are encoded as `any` + runtime casts instead of typed APIs.
evidence: `UpdateUrbitSectionConfig` accepts `mutateFn any` and `UrbitSectionMutator` validates by type assertion and stringly expected-type metadata.
`SysConfig.UpdateSection` stores `mutate func(*SysConfig, any) error` and casts `mutateFn.(func(*Section))` at runtime.
Runtime seam types continue propagating this shape via `UpdateUrbitSectionFn func(..., mutateFn any) error`, spreading dynamic mutation contracts across module boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Replace `any` section mutators with typed per-section methods or a generic API exposed at compile-time boundaries (for example, explicit `UpdateUrbitRuntime`, `UpdateUrbitNetwork`, and typed `UpdateSection[T]` wrappers) and remove string-based expected-type checks.

findings.summary: Runtime seam design is fragmented into overlapping bundles that duplicate capability declarations.
evidence: `RuntimeStartupOps` defines config/load/service callbacks, while `RuntimeConfigOps`, `RuntimeLoadOps`, and `RuntimeServiceOps` repeat subsets of the same callbacks.

Consumers like startram lifecycle flows require mixed capabilities (`UpdateConfTypedFn`, `StartContainerFn`, `LoadMCFn`, `SvcDeleteFn`), which are sourced from multiple partially-overlapping seam structs.
This creates multiple places to update when adding/changing one runtime operation and makes seam composition rules implicit rather than explicit.
findings.suggestion: Collapse overlapping op structs into a small number of cohesive capability interfaces (or one canonical runtime dependency object with focused sub-interfaces) and enforce composition through explicit constructor contracts used by workflow consumers.

findings.summary: Auth/unauth connection handling duplicates traversal logic, increasing inconsistency risk.
evidence: `ConnectionRegistry` repeats almost identical loops for both auth and unauth maps in multiple methods (`findMuConn`, `removeConnectionByConn`, `hasAuthSession`).
`removeClientFromOtherBucket` initializes `sourceMap` then conditionally reassigns it, reflecting brittle branch-oriented duplication around two bucket variants.
Session store code shows a similar repeated pattern for parallel collections (sys/docker copy/update paths), suggesting normalization helpers are underused.
findings.suggestion: Refactor registry operations to one bucket-iterator abstraction (built around `clientBuckets()`/`bucket(targetAuth)`) so lookup, cleanup, and removal use shared primitives; mirror that approach in session-store collection copy/update helpers to reduce branch duplication.

findings.summary: Config patch assembly uses panic for invalid specs while adjacent config update paths use recoverable errors.
evidence: configPatchFieldFromSpec panics when apply is nil or target accessors are missing.
confPatch parsing/apply helpers in config_update_schema.go are error-returning and propagate failures through normal error channels.
findings.suggestion: Replace panic branches in configPatchFieldFromSpec with returned construction errors (or startup-time validation errors) and propagate them through confPatch service initialization so malformed specs fail predictably without process crash.

findings.summary: Transition publish failures are treated as best-effort in importer flows but strict in ship workflow flows, causing inconsistent failure semantics for similar state events.
evidence: publishImportTransitionWithPolicy defaults to TransitionPublishBestEffort in importer.
runImportPhases and finalizeImportedPierReadiness log warnings and continue when publishImportStatus/publishImportError fail.
workflowRuntime in operations_runtime defaults TransitionErrorPolicy to TransitionPublishStrict and emitWorkflowTransition returns failures via HandleTransitionPublishError.
findings.suggestion: Define a single transition error-policy contract per lifecycle stage (critical vs non-critical events), then apply it consistently across importer and shipworkflow emitters; for critical importer phases, escalate publish failures instead of warning-only continuation.

findings.summary: Optional startup task failures are logged and dropped, making degraded startup states non-observable to callers.
evidence: runStartupSubsystem, startupOrchestrator.start, and startupOrchestrator.health call logOptionalStartupFailure then return nil for optional task errors.
main exits only on returned bootstrap error, so optional failures never surface as startup result state.
findings.suggestion: Return a structured startup result that includes optional-failure diagnostics (or aggregate optional errors into a typed warning error) so callers can make explicit decisions about degraded mode instead of relying only on logs.
